result:
There’s a specific kind of peace found only in the rhythmic clatter of a train. It’s a lullaby for the displaced, a steady, metallic heartbeat for those perpetually in-transit. Outside the smudged window, the world becomes a watercolor blur—green fields bleeding into the grey of suburban fences, telephone poles whipping past, stitching the sky to the earth. You are neither here nor there, suspended in the gentle custody of the in-between, a journey measured not in miles, but in the steady cadence of steel on steel.

This liminal space is a rare gift. It’s a pocket of time, free from the obligations of 'there' and not yet burdened by the demands of 'here'. No one expects anything of you. You are simply a passenger, an anonymous observer held in a temporary truce with time. The man across the aisle is asleep, his head resting against the cool, vibrating glass. A woman two rows up is lost in a book, her brow furrowed. Each person is a self-contained universe, a temporary constellation of strangers moving in the same direction but on entirely different journeys.

We don’t control the schedule or the tracks, only what we notice along the way. The destination is a fixed point, an eventual promise. But for these few hours, lulled by the motion and lost in the passing landscape, the journey is the only thing that feels truly real.
